These products included Linux kernel modules and BusyBox utilities distributed without providing the complete corresponding source code to users upon request. The software license required that recipients of the binary distributions receive offers for or direct provision of the source code, including any modifications made by the distributor. Cisco's product packaging and support mechanisms did not consistently include mechanisms for users to obtain the full source code, leading to multiple user complaints filed with the Free Software Foundation (FSF). This situation persisted across various product lines from 2003 onward, affecting consumer and enterprise-grade routers. The FSF documented specific instances where individuals requested source code for models such as the WRT54G series and received incomplete or delayed responses. Cisco's initial responses involved providing partial sources hosted on their website, but these were often outdated or missing components necessary for compilation and modification, as stipulated by the GPL. The issue gained prominence as part of broader industry scrutiny on embedded systems using open source components.
process_and_outcome: The Free Software Foundation initiated formal correspondence in 2008, demanding compliance. After negotiations, Cisco entered into a compliance settlement agreement with the FSF on September 17, 2009. Cisco committed to releasing complete source code for all GPL-licensed components in their products, establishing a dedicated GPL compliance portal on their website, and providing ongoing access to sources for products dating back to 2003. Cisco also agreed to train internal staff on GPL obligations and implemented processes to ensure future compliance across new product releases. No monetary penalties were imposed, but the settlement served as a public acknowledgment and model for other vendors.

example_name: BusyBox GPL Lawsuit against Best Buy
example_background: Best Buy Co., Inc., through its Insignia subsidiary, manufactured and sold Digital Picture Frames under the Insignia brand. These devices ran embedded Linux systems incorporating BusyBox, a collection of Unix utilities licensed under GPLv2. The firmware distributed on the devices did not include any offer or provision of corresponding source code for BusyBox or other GPL components such as the Linux kernel. Customers who reverse-engineered the devices discovered the GPL-licensed code but could not obtain official sources from Best Buy. This occurred across multiple models sold in 2006-2007, affecting thousands of units in retail channels. The lack of source availability prevented users from exercising rights to modify and redistribute the software as permitted by the license.
process_and_outcome: Software Freedom Law Center (SFLC), on behalf of BusyBox copyright holder Erik Andersen, filed a copyright infringement lawsuit against Best Buy and Samsung Electronics (OEM manufacturer) in February 2007 in the Southern District of New York. The court granted a preliminary injunction, and the parties settled in April 2007. Best Buy agreed to cease distribution of non-compliant products, provide source code for past sales upon request, and pay an undisclosed settlement amount estimated in the low six figures. The case set a precedent for embedded device GPL enforcement.

example_background: D-Link Corporation produced wireless routers and network-attached storage devices incorporating GPL-licensed software like the Linux kernel, BusyBox, and wireless drivers. Models such as the DIR-615 and DNS-323, sold from 2006-2008, distributed binaries without corresponding source code availability. Users requesting sources via the company's website received no response or incomplete tarballs missing essential files. This affected consumer products widely available in North America and Europe, where embedded GPL usage was common but compliance documentation was absent from user manuals and support sites.
process_and_outcome: In July 2008, SFLC sent a demand letter to D-Link. Following non-response, a lawsuit was filed in the Eastern District of New York. D-Link settled in November 2008, releasing complete sources for all affected products, appointing a GPL compliance officer, and paying damages. The settlement included commitments to ongoing compliance audits.

example_background: Canonical Ltd. maintains the Ubuntu Linux distribution, which incorporates extensive GPL-licensed software. All releases provide complete source code through the Ubuntu package archive at packages.ubuntu.com and launchpad.net, including Debian source packages (.dscs), orig tarballs, and diff.gz patches. Users can download sources for every binary package, with build dependencies listed and reproducible builds encouraged. This covers desktop, server, and embedded variants like Ubuntu Core.
process_and_outcome: Canonical's model has sustained over 20 years without compliance disputes, serving as a standard for Debian-based distributions. The company actively participates in free software communities, contributing patches and maintaining compliance documentation.

example_name: Intel Corporation GPL Compliance for Firmware
example_background: Intel develops firmware and Linux kernel modules for chipsets, network adapters, and management engines. Products like Intel PRO/1000 network drivers and microcode updates are released with full GPLv2 source code on kernel.org and Intel's open source site. Complete git repositories include history, makefiles, and documentation, allowing compilation against various kernel versions.
process_and_outcome: Intel's proactive release practices, ongoing since the early 2000s, have prevented enforcement actions and earned endorsements from Linux kernel maintainers. The company maintains a dedicated open source compliance team.

example_name: Linksys GPL Compliance After Cisco Acquisition
example_background: Following Cisco's 2008 settlement, Linksys implemented a GPL compliance portal providing sources for WRT series routers and other devices. Users access tarballs, git repos, and changelogs for Linux-based firmware, including custom builds for openWrt community.
process_and_outcome: Post-settlement, Linksys maintained compliance, releasing sources with each firmware update, demonstrating sustained adherence.

example_background: Monsoon Media sold HDTV recorders using Linux and BusyBox without source code provision. Devices distributed 2006-2007 lacked GPL compliance info.
process_and_outcome: SFLC sued in 2007; Monsoon settled, released sources, donated to charity, and ceased non-compliant sales.

example_background: Samsung Electronics incorporated BusyBox, a software utility licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), into various products including televisions and Blu-ray players without providing the required source code to users. BusyBox is a collection of Unix utilities designed for embedded systems and is commonly used in consumer electronics. The Software Freedom Conservancy, acting on behalf of BusyBox developers, identified that Samsung distributed modified versions of BusyBox in firmware without making the corresponding source code available as stipulated by the GPLv2. This distribution occurred across multiple product lines over several years, affecting consumers who purchased the devices. The lack of source code availability prevented users from studying, modifying, or redistributing the software as permitted under the license terms. Samsung's practices involved shipping products with embedded GPL-licensed software but failing to comply with the copyleft requirements that mandate source code distribution upon request or alongside the binaries.
process_and_outcome: The Software Freedom Conservancy sent notices of violation to Samsung in 2009. Samsung initially did not respond adequately. After continued engagement, Samsung agreed in 2010 to a settlement that included publishing source code for affected products on their open source website, implementing internal compliance processes for GPL software, and paying damages. No further litigation occurred after the settlement.
involved_parties: Software Freedom Conservancy (enforcer on behalf of BusyBox developers)

example_background: Cisco Systems, through its Linksys division, distributed wireless routers and other networking equipment containing Linux kernel and BusyBox components licensed under the GPLv2. These products included firmware with GPL-licensed code, but Cisco did not provide the source code to end users or upon request for an extended period. The devices were widely sold to consumers, and the embedded software enabled core functionalities like routing and network management. The Software Freedom Law Center identified the non-compliance in 2008 after receiving reports from users unable to access source code. This case highlighted issues in the networking hardware industry where proprietary firmware often incorporated open source components without meeting license obligations.
process_and_outcome: Cisco received a notice of violation in December 2008. Following negotiations, Cisco settled in May 2009 by releasing source code for numerous products, establishing a GPL compliance team, publishing a compliance policy, and publishing historical source code archives. The settlement ensured ongoing compliance without court involvement.

example_name: D-Link GPL Violation by Software Freedom Conservancy
example_background: D-Link Corporation distributed routers and wireless access points incorporating BusyBox and other GPL-licensed software without providing corresponding source code. Products such as the DIR-615 and DGL-4500 models were affected, sold internationally to consumers. The embedded software managed network functions, but users were unable to obtain source code despite license requirements. The Software Freedom Conservancy investigated after user complaints and confirmed distribution of modified GPL code without source availability over multiple firmware versions spanning 2007-2010.
process_and_outcome: Notices were sent in 2010. After non-response, the Conservancy filed a federal lawsuit in 2011. D-Link settled in December 2011, agreeing to release all relevant source code, implement compliance procedures, and pay damages. The case was the first federal court GPL enforcement action filed.

example_name: Monsoon Media VLC GPL Compliance After Notice
example_background: Monsoon Media, creators of the Popcorn Hour media player, shipped devices with VideoLAN Client (VLC) software licensed under GPLv2 without initially providing source code. The product was marketed for home media playback and included VLC for multimedia handling. VideoLAN organization identified the distribution in 2009 and noted that firmware updates continued without source code availability, affecting users' ability to modify or rebuild the software.
process_and_outcome: VideoLAN sent a notice of violation in November 2009. This was the first notice from VideoLAN to Monsoon. Within 30 days, Monsoon complied by publishing the VLC source code on their website and firmware download pages. No further action was required, and the license was reinstated per GPLv2 terms.

example_name: VMware GPL Violation Settlement with Conservancy
example_background: VMware included BusyBox in ESX Server virtualization products distributed to enterprise customers without providing source code. The software was part of the console OS for server management. Software Freedom Conservancy detected the inclusion in versions from 2003-2007 through code analysis and user reports. VMware's products were used in data centers worldwide, and the lack of source code distribution persisted across multiple releases.
process_and_outcome: Notices sent in 2008. VMware settled in 2009 by releasing source code for affected products, paying damages, and committing to GPL compliance policies. The settlement covered historical and future distributions.

example_name: Best Buy Insignia Products GPL Enforcement
example_background: Best Buy's Insignia brand sold Blu-ray players and networked devices containing BusyBox and GPL-licensed software without source code availability. Models like NS-BRDVD were distributed in retail stores. Software Freedom Conservancy found embedded GPL code in firmware during 2009 investigations, noting no source provision despite widespread sales.
process_and_outcome: Notice sent in 2009. Best Buy responded by halting non-compliant distributions and arranging source code release through partners. Full compliance achieved post-notice without litigation.

example_name: Pace Digital Television Set-Top Boxes GPL Case
example_background: Pace plc manufactured digital set-top boxes for cable and satellite providers, embedding Linux and BusyBox under GPLv2 without source code distribution. Devices were deployed to millions of subscribers in the US and Europe. Conservancy identified violations in 2008 through firmware reverse engineering.
process_and_outcome: Notices in 2008 led to settlement in 2009. Pace released source code archives, implemented compliance training, and paid fees. Ongoing cooperation ensured future adherence.

example_background: Openmoko released the Neo1973 smartphone entirely under GPLv2 and other free licenses, providing full source code for hardware designs, firmware, and applications from launch in 2007. The project aimed to create an open platform for mobile devices, distributing software via downloads and including source in shipments.
process_and_outcome: No violations occurred. The project maintained continuous source availability, enabling community contributions and modifications. It served as a model for open hardware-software integration.

example_name: Harman International JVC Receivers GPL Settlement
example_background: Harman International's JVC-branded network receivers included GPL-licensed uClibc and BusyBox in firmware without source code. Products were sold in consumer electronics markets from 2008 onward. Software Freedom Conservancy notified after code audits.
process_and_outcome: Notice in 2010 prompted Harman to publish source code for all affected models and adopt internal GPL policies by 2011.

example_name: BusyBox GPL Violations - Multiple Enforcement Actions
example_background: BusyBox, a lightweight set of Unix utilities licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL) version 2, has been central to numerous enforcement actions since the early 2000s. Companies embedded BusyBox in embedded systems, routers, and firmware without providing required source code or binary distribution notices as stipulated by the GPL. For instance, in embedded devices from manufacturers like Linksys (Cisco), Netgear, and others, BusyBox binaries were included in proprietary firmware without accompanying GPL-compliant source code availability. The Software Freedom Conservancy (SFC) and original author Erik Andersen pursued cases where violations persisted after initial notices. One notable pattern involved network-attached storage (NAS) devices and digital video recorders (DVRs) where modified BusyBox was statically linked into executables, obscuring license obligations. These incidents demonstrated repeated failures to adhere to copyleft requirements after initial distribution, often requiring multiple notifications before compliance was achieved.
process_and_outcome: SFC sent cease-and-desist letters starting in 2007, leading to settlements with over 15 companies. Outcomes included public source code releases, compliance programs, and donations to the SFC. For example, Xyratex agreed to a settlement in 2009 after failing to comply post-notification, releasing sources and establishing a compliance officer. No court judgments were needed as most settled out-of-court, but enforcement continued into the 2010s with cases like Verkada in 2020.
involved_parties: Various companies (e.g., Cisco/Linksys, Netgear, Xyratex, Verkada)

example_name: Red Hat Cure53 AGPL Audit and Compliance
example_background: Red Hat engaged Cure53, a security consultancy, to audit its open source components for compliance with the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL), which applies to server-side software and requires source code provision for network users. This proactive audit examined Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) and related products to ensure all AGPL components met source disclosure and modification notice requirements. The process identified minor issues, which were addressed promptly, demonstrating a structured approach to license reinstatement after any potential lapses.
process_and_outcome: The 2016 audit report confirmed overall compliance, with recommendations implemented, resulting in no enforcement actions. Red Hat published the report publicly, reinforcing its open source compliance leadership.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPLv3)

example_background: Google's Android operating system incorporates extensive GPL and LGPL licensed components from the Linux kernel and other projects. To manage compliance, Google established a dedicated team and public source code repositories (AOSP) where kernel sources and modifications are released regularly. Following community feedback on delays in source availability for certain devices, Google formalized processes to notify partners and provide sources within timelines, addressing provisional reinstatement clauses by responding to violation reports.
process_and_outcome: Google publishes kernel sources for all official Android devices on source.android.com, with compliance upheld through ongoing releases and responses to developer queries. No major enforcement actions have been taken against Google for Android GPL issues.

example_background: Samsung included BusyBox in numerous printer firmware and embedded systems without source code or license notices, violating GPLv2 terms. Community scans identified affected models like multifunction printers sold globally. After initial detection around 2008, Samsung continued distribution briefly before remediation.
process_and_outcome: Settlement with Software Freedom Conservancy in 2010 required source code publication for 50+ products, compliance training, and a donation. Sources were hosted on Samsung's open source portal.

example_background: Wind River, a provider of real-time operating systems, incorporated GPL code into its VxWorks products used in aerospace and automotive applications without proper source disclosure. Violations were identified through systematic scans by enforcement groups in the mid-2000s.
process_and_outcome: 2009 settlement with gpl-violations.org led to source code release, internal policy updates, and public commitment to GPL compliance in future releases.

example_name: IBM Open Source License Compliance Framework
example_background: IBM developed an enterprise-wide open source compliance program covering licenses like GPL, including automated scanning tools and legal reviews for all software products. This framework ensures prompt response to notices and source availability for qualifying components.
process_and_outcome: The program has prevented violations, with IBM maintaining a public compliance portal and contributing to community enforcement efforts, achieving sustained adherence.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: IBM Open Source License Compliance Policy

example_name: BusyBox GPL Lawsuit by Software Freedom Law Center
example_background: In 2007, the Software Freedom Law Center initiated legal action against multiple companies for distributing modified versions of the BusyBox software utility, which is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2). BusyBox is a lightweight set of Unix utilities commonly embedded in firmware and devices. The defendants included firms such as XimpleWare, Samsung Electronics America, and JVC. These companies had incorporated BusyBox into their products, including network-attached storage devices and digital video recorders, but did not provide the required source code to users or make it publicly available upon request. The lawsuits highlighted instances where firmware images contained BusyBox binaries without accompanying source code, and modifications were made without distributing the corresponding source under the same license terms. This case was part of a broader series of enforcement efforts targeting embedded systems manufacturers who utilized open-source components without fulfilling distribution obligations.
process_and_outcome: The Software Freedom Law Center filed separate lawsuits in US federal courts. XimpleWare settled by agreeing to comply with GPLv2 terms and pay damages. Samsung settled with an undisclosed agreement that included source code release. JVC discontinued the product and settled. The cases resulted in settlements that enforced source code publication and monetary payments, setting precedents for GPL compliance in embedded systems.

example_background: Cisco Systems faced allegations in 2008 from the Free Software Foundation for distributing products that incorporated GPL-covered software, including components under GPLv2, without providing the required source code. Affected products included Linksys routers and other networking equipment where Linux kernel modules and utilities licensed under GPLv2 and LGPLv2.1 were modified and distributed in binary form only. Cisco had acquired Linksys, inheriting products with embedded open-source software. The company was distributing firmware updates and device images without making corresponding source code available through their websites or upon user request, as stipulated by the licenses. This involved numerous models of wireless routers, access points, and VoIP phones spanning several years of product releases.
process_and_outcome: Cisco entered into a compliance agreement with the Free Software Foundation, committing to appoint a GPL compliance officer, publish source code for affected products on a dedicated website, and implement processes for ongoing open-source license compliance. No monetary fines were publicly disclosed, but the settlement required extensive remediation efforts including backfilling source code for legacy products.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GNU Lesser General Public License version 2.1 (LGPLv2.1)

example_background: In 2008, VMware was investigated by the Software Freedom Law Center for using GPL-covered code from the Linux kernel, BusyBox, and other utilities under GPLv2 in its virtualization products, including VMware Workstation and ESX Server. The company had linked proprietary modules to GPL-licensed kernel code without releasing source code for those modules or providing complete sources for the GPL components used. This occurred in products distributed to customers worldwide, where virtual machine monitors incorporated modified GPL code without proper attribution or source availability. The allegations focused on kernel modules and user-space tools that were part of VMware's guest operating system support.
process_and_outcome: VMware discontinued the non-compliant products, removed GPL code from its offerings, and settled with the complainants. The company issued public statements on changing its approach to open-source usage and began providing source code for compliant components in other products.

example_background: D-Link, a networking equipment manufacturer, distributed wireless routers and access points incorporating BusyBox and other GPLv2-licensed software starting around 2005. Models such as the DIR-615 and DGL-3420 contained modified BusyBox binaries in their firmware without providing source code on their support sites or responding to requests. The firmware also included Linux kernel components under GPLv2. Users and developers discovered the embedded GPL code through reverse engineering and binwalk analysis, revealing unlicensed modifications and missing copyright notices in some cases. This affected numerous product lines sold globally.
process_and_outcome: Following complaints to the Free Software Foundation and public disclosure, D-Link began publishing source code archives for many products on their website. Some models required firmware updates to restore compliance, and the company faced community pressure but no formal lawsuit.

example_background: Netgear routers such as the WGRv9 and ReadyNAS devices from 2006-2008 included BusyBox under GPLv2 and LGPLv2 components in firmware without source code availability. Modified versions stripped copyright headers, and support sites lacked download links for sources. Community researchers identified the issues via firmware extraction, leading to complaints.
process_and_outcome: Netgear settled informally by publishing source code tarballs for affected models and updating compliance processes. No lawsuit proceeded, but public listings on gpl-violations.org documented the remediation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GNU Lesser General Public License version 2 (LGPLv2)

example_name: Wind River Systems GPL Enforcement by FSF
example_background: In 2010, the Free Software Foundation pursued Wind River Systems for distributing embedded Linux products under GPLv2 without source code. Products like Wind River Linux included kernel and BusyBox modifications for automotive and aerospace uses, distributed to OEMs without sources.
process_and_outcome: Wind River entered a compliance agreement, released sources for numerous products, and implemented internal tracking for GPL components. The settlement was publicized as a success for collaborative enforcement.

example_background: Canonical, developer of Ubuntu Linux, systematically provides source code for all distributions including main, universe, and multiverse repositories under GPLv2 and LGPLv2.1. Sources are hosted on Launchpad with package manifests, build logs, and Debian packaging details. This covers desktop, server, and embedded variants with modifications tracked via changelogs.
process_and_outcome: Canonical's model has been audited by the community and regulators without compliance findings. It contributes upstream and maintains reproducibility, earning recognition for best practices.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GNU Lesser General Public License version 2.1 (LGPLv2.1)

example_name: Monsoon Media GPL Lawsuit over Popcorn Hour
example_background: In 2010, Software Freedom Law Center sued Monsoon Media for distributing Popcorn Hour media players with BusyBox and other GPLv2 code. Firmware for models like A-100 and A-110 used modified GPL software without sources, affecting thousands of units sold.
process_and_outcome: The court ruled in favor of plaintiffs in 2011, ordering compliance and damages. Monsoon ceased operations shortly after, marking a significant judicial enforcement of GPL.

example_name: GitHub DMCA Takedown of DMCA-512 GitHub Repository
example_background: In December 2017, GitHub received a DMCA takedown notice from the Motion Picture Association of America (MPAA) targeting the 'DMCA-512' GitHub repository. This repository was maintained by GitHub staff to document and archive notices received under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) Section 512 safe harbor provisions. The repository contained publicly available DMCA notices sent to GitHub, which included links to hosted content alleged to infringe copyrights. The MPAA claimed that hosting these notices violated their copyrights by reproducing portions of their notices without permission. GitHub complied with the takedown by removing the repository, sparking widespread discussion in the tech community about the implications of DMCA notices being used against platforms documenting their own compliance processes. The incident highlighted tensions between copyright enforcement mechanisms and transparency efforts by online service providers.
process_and_outcome: GitHub processed the DMCA notice and removed the repository on December 28, 2017. GitHub staff publicly noted the irony and issued counter-notices where applicable, but the repository remained offline. The event led to community backlash, with developers forking the repo elsewhere and GitHub eventually restoring a version after legal review. No further enforcement actions were taken against GitHub.

example_background: The Lumen Database, formerly known as the Chilling Effects project, partners with GitHub and other platforms to archive DMCA notices for research and transparency purposes. GitHub contributes anonymized DMCA notices to this database, which is hosted at Harvard Law School. This practice began as part of efforts to study the impact of copyright takedown notices on free expression. GitHub's participation involves systematically forwarding notices received under its DMCA policy to Lumen, ensuring that data is processed without revealing confidential user information. The database has collected millions of notices since 2002, providing empirical data on notice-and-takedown practices across the internet industry.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing collaboration with no disputes; the database continues to operate and publish annual reports on DMCA trends. GitHub's contributions have supported academic studies and policy recommendations without incurring legal challenges.

example_name: RIAA Mass DMCA Notices to GitHub Repositories
example_background: In 2017-2018, the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA) sent multiple DMCA takedown notices to GitHub targeting repositories containing YouTube downloaders and playlist tools. These open-source projects, such as 'youtube-dl' forks, were accused of facilitating copyright infringement by allowing users to download music videos from YouTube. GitHub processed hundreds of such notices, affecting thousands of repositories, including those with legitimate uses like archiving public domain content or offline viewing tools. The actions raised concerns about overreach in DMCA applications to software code itself.
process_and_outcome: GitHub removed targeted files and repositories per DMCA policy. Developers filed counter-notices, leading to some restorations. The 'youtube-dl' project was temporarily suspended but reinstated after legal threats from the Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF). RIAA continued sending notices into 2019.

example_name: Dice.com DMCA Policy Enforcement Against Job Scrapers
example_background: Dice.com, a tech job board, issued DMCA takedown notices to GitHub in 2016 targeting repositories that scraped and republished its job listings. The scrapers were open-source tools used by job aggregators to collect data from multiple sites, including Dice's proprietary listings protected by copyright. GitHub received and processed these notices, removing the offending code and data files. The incident underscored how DMCA is used by data providers to protect scraped content, even when the underlying jobs are publicly accessible.
process_and_outcome: GitHub actioned the takedowns swiftly, with no counter-notices filed. The repositories were deleted, and Dice continued monitoring for similar scrapers.

example_name: GitHub's Own DMCA Counter-Notice Process in Sega v. GitHub
example_background: In 2021, Sega Enterprises issued DMCA notices to GitHub targeting repositories hosting emulators and ROMs for vintage Sega games, such as YabaSanshiro. These projects involved reverse-engineered code to run Sega Saturn games on modern hardware. Sega claimed the code infringed their copyrights in the original games. GitHub removed the repositories but developers filed counter-notices asserting fair use and interoperability exceptions under copyright law. The case drew attention to DMCA's application to emulation software.
process_and_outcome: GitHub notified Sega of the counter-notices. Sega filed a lawsuit in U.S. District Court (ND California) against the developers but did not pursue GitHub directly. Repositories were restored after 10-14 days per DMCA rules when no lawsuit was filed against developers.

example_name: Automattic v. GitHub DMCA Over Git Updater Plugin
example_background: In 2019, Automattic (WordPress.com) sent a DMCA notice to GitHub for the 'Git Updater' plugin repository, which allowed WordPress users to update plugins directly from GitHub repositories, bypassing the official WordPress plugin directory. Automattic argued this circumvented their review process and potentially distributed unvetted code containing their copyrighted elements. GitHub processed the notice amid debates on whether plugin update mechanisms constitute infringement.
process_and_outcome: Repository was taken down. The plugin author filed a counter-notice, leading to restoration after Automattic did not file suit within the statutory period. The incident prompted discussions on DMCA misuse against open-source tools.

example_name: GitHub Transparency Report on DMCA Notices
example_background: GitHub publishes biannual transparency reports detailing the volume and nature of DMCA notices received, government requests, and takedown actions. These reports cover data from 2012 onward, showing GitHub processed over 10,000 DMCA notices in some periods, primarily targeting repositories with pirated software, media, or tools enabling infringement. The reports emphasize GitHub's commitment to due process, including counter-notice handling and abuse reporting.
process_and_outcome: Reports are issued regularly without regulatory intervention, serving as a model for platform accountability. They have informed policy discussions at W3C and EU levels on intermediary liability.

example_name: MPAA DMCA Against Popcorn Time Repositories
example_background: In 2015, the MPAA targeted multiple GitHub repositories hosting Popcorn Time, an open-source BitTorrent client with a Netflix-like interface for streaming pirated movies. Notices claimed the client and its code directly infringed copyrights by facilitating unauthorized distribution. GitHub suspended dozens of forks and mirrors, affecting a global network of developers.
process_and_outcome: GitHub terminated accounts and repositories per notices. Popcorn Time developers migrated to other hosts. No lawsuits against GitHub, but the action disrupted the project temporarily.

example_background: Odebrecht, a Brazilian construction giant, operated a dedicated bribery division called the Division of Structured Operations from 2001 to 2016. This unit facilitated over $788 million in bribes to political leaders and officials in Brazil, Venezuela, Mexico, Peru, Mozambique, and other countries to obtain public infrastructure contracts. Payments were made through offshore accounts, fake consulting contracts, and cash deliveries, influencing elections and policy decisions. The scheme involved high-level politicians and state-owned oil company executives, distorting public procurement processes across Latin America and beyond.
process_and_outcome: Brazilian Federal Police Operation Car Wash (Lava Jato) uncovered the scheme through plea deals and evidence from executives. Odebrecht admitted liability in US, Brazilian, and Swiss courts, paying $3.5 billion in penalties in a 2016 global settlement. Multiple executives received prison sentences, and numerous politicians faced impeachment or trials.
involved_parties: Swiss Office of the Attorney General

example_background: Alstom, a French power and transport company, made over $300 million in corrupt payments to officials in countries including Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Taiwan, and the Bahamas between 2000 and 2011 to secure power, transmission, and rail contracts. Payments were routed through agents and advisors with inflated commissions, often linked to government-owned utilities. US investigations revealed inadequate internal controls and deliberate concealment of transactions.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ charged Alstom with FCPA violations in 2014, resulting in a $772 million penalty—the largest ever against a non-US company at the time. Subsidiaries pleaded guilty, and executives faced individual charges. A three-year monitorship was imposed.

example_background: ABB, a Swiss-Swedish engineering firm, through its US subsidiary, made improper payments totaling $21.2 million to officials in South Africa from 2002 to 2007 to influence contracts for boiler installations at power stations. Additional payments occurred in Egypt. The company used agents to funnel funds disguised as legitimate services.
process_and_outcome: SEC and DOJ settled with ABB in 2010 for $56.4 million, including $315 million disgorgement. ABB improved its compliance program and faced ongoing SEC reporting requirements.

example_background: Walmart faced allegations that from 2002 to 2005, its Mexico subsidiary executives authorized $24 million in suspect payments to Mexican officials to expedite permits for store openings in high-traffic areas. Internal investigations initially downplayed issues, but a 2012 New York Times report prompted deeper probes into third-party intermediary use.
process_and_outcome: DOJ and SEC investigated for years, leading to a 2019 $282 million settlement with Walmart for FCPA violations. The company enhanced global anti-corruption controls and self-reported additional matters.

example_background: Rolls-Royce, a British aerospace and defense firm, engaged in bribery schemes from 2000 to 2013 in China, Indonesia, Russia, Thailand, Brazil, and India, paying hundreds of millions through agents to secure engine and power contracts. Intermediaries received success fees tied to contract awards, with payments hidden in offshore structures.
process_and_outcome: UK Serious Fraud Office, US DOJ, and Brazilian authorities coordinated, resulting in a $1 billion deferred prosecution agreement in 2017. Rolls-Royce avoided conviction by implementing reforms and paying penalties across jurisdictions.

example_background: Embraer, a Brazilian aircraft manufacturer, used advisors to pay approximately $11 million in commissions to a Ghanaian official from 2007 to 2009 to facilitate a $50 million military cargo plane sale. Additional schemes involved Dominican Republic officials for executive jet purchases.
process_and_outcome: SEC and DOJ settled in 2016 for $107 million, including $40.5 million profit disgorgement. Embraer enhanced compliance and faced a two-year monitorship.

example_background: Siemens AG, a major German engineering and electronics conglomerate, engaged in a widespread scheme of making improper payments to government officials and others to secure contracts across multiple countries including Argentina, Venezuela, Greece, and Nigeria. The practice involved the use of slush funds, shell companies, and cash payments facilitated through intermediaries. Between 2001 and 2007, the company recorded over 4,500 individual transactions totaling approximately €1.4 billion in suspicious payments. These actions were part of a corporate culture where business units maintained hidden accounts for such purposes, often with the knowledge of senior management. The scheme spanned infrastructure projects, telecommunications, and power generation sectors, affecting public procurement processes globally.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Department of Justice and SEC investigated under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, leading to Siemens pleading guilty to conspiracy to violate FCPA books and records provisions. The company paid $450 million in criminal penalties to U.S. authorities and €1.6 billion in fines and disgorgement to German authorities, totaling over $1.6 billion. Siemens overhauled its compliance program, appointed a compliance monitor, and terminated numerous executives.

example_background: Walmart, the multinational retail corporation, faced allegations of inadequate internal controls in its international operations, particularly in Mexico, Brazil, China, and India. From 2000 to 2011, Walmart subsidiaries allegedly made or authorized improper payments to local government officials to expedite construction permits and zoning approvals for new stores. In Mexico alone, over 24 separate instances involved third-party intermediaries who passed payments totaling millions to officials. The company continued operations despite internal audit findings of suspicious activity, prioritizing business expansion over addressing compliance gaps.
process_and_outcome: DOJ and SEC conducted a multi-year investigation. Walmart entered into a deferred prosecution agreement, paid $137 million in penalties and disgorgement, and committed to enhancing its anti-corruption compliance program with independent monitoring for two years. No criminal charges were brought against executives.

example_background: Odebrecht S.A., a Brazilian construction giant, and its petrochemical affiliate Braskem S.A. operated a dedicated Division of Structured Operations from 2001 to 2016 to manage off-the-books bribe payments totaling over $788 million to politicians and officials across Latin America, Africa, and elsewhere. Payments were disguised through a complex system of offshore shell companies and false contracts. The scheme secured over $3.3 billion in contracts and influenced elections and legislation in countries like Brazil, Venezuela, Mexico, and Mozambique.
process_and_outcome: The companies admitted liability in U.S., Brazilian, and Swiss proceedings, paying a combined $3.5 billion in penalties ($2.6 billion from Odebrecht). Odebrecht faced a 10-year monitorship and bankruptcy restructuring. Braskem paid $250 million to U.S. authorities.
involved_parties: Swiss Office of the Attorney General

example_name: Alcoa World Alumina and Alcoa Inc. Bribery
example_background: Alcoa World Alumina LLC and Alcoa Inc., U.S.-based aluminum producers, along with subsidiaries, engaged in a scheme from 1989 to 2005 involving payments of over $69 million in bribes to officials at a Bahraini government-owned aluminum smelter. Payments were routed through retained consultants who received inflated fees, securing over $5.1 billion in alumina supply contracts. The company knowingly used intermediaries with connections to officials despite red flags.
process_and_outcome: DOJ and SEC settlement resulted in $384 million in penalties, including $209 million criminal fine and $14 million civil penalty. Alcoa implemented compliance improvements without admitting liability.

example_background: Baker Hughes Inc., an oilfield services company, made over $4.7 million in improper payments via agents to officials in Kazakhstan, Russia, Yemen, and others from 1999 to 2004. Payments influenced customs clearance and contract awards. Internal controls were weak, and suspicious payments were not adequately monitored.
process_and_outcome: Company paid $44 million in penalties to DOJ and SEC, retained compliance consultant, and certified improvements in anti-corruption program.

example_background: BHP Billiton Limited, an Anglo-Australian mining company, sponsored a $10 million community event in Belgium during 2008 World Cup bid discussions to influence FIFA officials regarding the 2022 World Cup hosting decision. The event was not properly authorized under internal policies, and records understated its purpose.
process_and_outcome: DOJ resolved with $15 million civil penalty under FCPA books and records provisions. No criminal charges; BHP enhanced compliance controls.

example_background: Walmart's Mexican subsidiary, Walmart de Mexico y Centroamerica, faced allegations of authorizing payments to local officials to expedite store permits and zoning approvals in the early 2000s. example_background: ABB Ltd, a Swiss-Swedish engineering firm, made improper payments to Egyptian officials between 2001 and 2009 to secure contracts for power and automation projects. The company opened over 200 stores during this period, benefiting from accelerated approvals that bypassed standard regulatory reviews.

In 2019, Walmart settled by paying $282 million in penalties and disgorgement, avoiding criminal charges through a non-prosecution agreement. example_background: Alcoa Inc., a U.S. aluminum producer, through subsidiaries, facilitated over $69 million in bribe payments from 1980s to 2000s to officials at state-owned entities in Bahrain, Brazil, and Kuwait to secure alumina supply and smelter construction contracts worth billions. example_background: Following prior AML issues, HSBC implemented a robust anti-corruption program post-2012, including mandatory training on FCPA and internal policies covering gifts, hospitality, third-party due diligence, red flags like unusual payment requests, and accurate record-keeping. Internal compliance flagged issues, but business pressures led to approvals. This incident highlighted risks in government IT contracts where resellers act as intermediaries.
process_and_outcome: The DOJ and SEC resolved the matter in 2022 with a $23 million civil penalty and a three-year deferred prosecution agreement. Oracle committed to ongoing remediation, including global third-party risk management and anti-corruption training. No admission of liability was required due to self-reporting.

The company lacked sufficient controls over channel partners, with executives pressuring sales teams to meet targets through improper means.

The SEC imposed parallel fines. SAP overhauled partner vetting and monitoring processes globally.

example_background: Odebrecht S.A., a Brazilian construction conglomerate, and its petrochemical subsidiary Braskem S.A. operated a sophisticated bribery network known as the 'Division of Structured Operations' from 2001 to 2016. Funds were laundered through offshore accounts, fake contracts with vendors, and cash payments, involving a dedicated unit that automated bribe tracking via encrypted systems. This occurred despite the companies having vendor relationships that facilitated the illicit payments.
process_and_outcome: Following Operation Car Wash investigations by Brazilian authorities, Odebrecht and Braskem pleaded guilty in U.S. court in 2016, agreeing to pay a combined $3.5 billion penalty—the largest in FCPA history. Penalties were split among Brazil, Switzerland, and the U.S. The companies enhanced compliance measures, including vendor audits and anti-corruption clauses in contracts. Numerous executives received prison sentences.

example_background: Microsoft's Irish subsidiary was involved in a data processing arrangement where personal data of European users was transferred to the U.S. under Privacy Shield. Intermediaries received commissions that were not adequately documented or justified as legitimate business expenses. This occurred across various divisions of the company, involving senior executives who approved or overlooked the arrangements as standard practice for winning international business.

example_name: Oracle America v. Google (Android-Java API Case)
example_background: Oracle acquired Sun Microsystems in 2010, gaining rights to Java intellectual property. Google developed the Android operating system using a set of Java application programming interfaces (APIs) for compatibility with existing developer code. Oracle initiated a copyright infringement lawsuit against Google in 2010, claiming that Google's replication of 37 Java API packages constituted unauthorized copying. The case progressed through multiple phases, involving questions of fair use and the scope of copyright protection for functional code elements. Google defended its actions by arguing that the APIs were necessary for interoperability and constituted transformative fair use in creating a new mobile platform. The dispute centered on whether Google's independent implementation of the declaring code and structure of the APIs infringed Oracle's copyrights.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. District Court initially ruled in Google's favor on fair use in 2016, but the Federal Circuit reversed in 2018. The U.S. Supreme Court granted certiorari and in 2021 affirmed that Google's use qualified as fair use, resolving the case without liability. No damages were awarded to Oracle.

example_name: Sun Microsystems v. Microsoft (Java Licensing Dispute)
example_background: In the mid-1990s, Sun Microsystems licensed its Java technology to Microsoft for integration into Windows. Microsoft implemented Java in Internet Explorer but modified the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) to favor its own platform-specific extensions, diverging from Sun's 'write once, run anywhere' principle. Sun alleged that Microsoft altered Java compatibility test suites and suppressed full cross-platform functionality to direct developers toward Windows-specific APIs. This led to a 1997 lawsuit where Sun sought to enforce the licensing agreement and prevent Microsoft from further distribution of its modified Java runtime environment.
process_and_outcome: The parties settled in 1998 with Microsoft paying Sun $20 million in retroactive royalties and agreeing to include a clean-room implementation of Sun's Java software in Windows for two years. Microsoft later removed Java support from consumer Windows versions.

example_background: An anonymous plaintiff using the pseudonym 'Supernova' filed a copyright infringement lawsuit against GitHub in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California in 2023. The suit alleged that GitHub's Copilot tool, an AI-powered code completion service trained on publicly available code from GitHub repositories, generated code that directly copied copyrighted material from open-source projects without attribution or permission. Supernova claimed that Copilot outputted verbatim snippets from licensed repositories, including those under restrictive licenses prohibiting commercial use or requiring specific notices. The case highlighted tensions between AI training data practices and developer licensing expectations on code-sharing platforms.
process_and_outcome: GitHub filed a motion to dismiss in January 2024, arguing fair use and that the claims failed to meet pleading standards. The court partially denied the motion in July 2024, allowing certain breach of contract and unjust enrichment claims to proceed while dismissing others. The case remains ongoing.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California

example_background: GitHub has faced repeated waves of DMCA takedown notices targeting legitimate open-source repositories, often from competitors or entities seeking to suppress rival projects. In response, GitHub issued counter-notices under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act's safe harbor provisions, restoring content after claimants failed to pursue court actions. Notable patterns emerged with coordinated filings against cryptocurrency-related repos and security research tools.
process_and_outcome: GitHub successfully restored numerous repositories via counter-notices, such as in 2018-2019 crypto repo takedowns and 2021 abuse patterns. No further litigation ensued from claimants, affirming GitHub's compliance with DMCA processes.

example_background: In 2003, Microsoft sent a cease-and-desist letter to a fan site named 'MikeRoweSoft.com,' a parody referencing Windows XP's 'mspaint.exe' creator Mike Rowe. The site aggregated Microsoft-related content. Microsoft initiated a trademark infringement claim over the domain name's similarity to 'Microsoft.' The domain owner filed a UDRP complaint with the World Intellectual Property Organization, turning it into a defensive trademark dispute.
process_and_outcome: The parties settled amicably; Microsoft withdrew claims, and the owner transferred the domain voluntarily. No formal litigation proceeded.

example_background: The BusyBox project, licensed under GPL, pursued multiple lawsuits against embedded device manufacturers who incorporated BusyBox into proprietary firmware without providing required source code. Erik Andersen and Rob Landley initiated actions against companies shipping Linux-based routers and devices. Defendants included network hardware firms that linked BusyBox statically into binaries, violating GPL copyleft terms.
process_and_outcome: Settlements resulted in public source code releases and compliance agreements. For example, XimpleWare settled in 2009 by publishing sources; Best Buy agreed to remediation in 2008.

example_background: In 2020, the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agency hosted repositories on GitHub for internal tools related to data processing. Following public scrutiny over ICE's role in family separations, GitHub reviewed the account under its Terms of Service prohibiting content facilitating human rights abuses. GitHub requested that ICE remove or privatize specific repositories.
process_and_outcome: ICE challenged the request, prompting GitHub (a Microsoft subsidiary) to engage in dialogue. GitHub enforced its policy by restricting public access, with ICE complying by privatizing repos. No formal litigation occurred.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GitHub Terms of Service (Acceptable Use Policy)

example_background: Artifex Software, creators of the Ghostscript PDF interpreter under AGPL license, sued Hancom Office North America in 2016. Hancom incorporated Ghostscript into its WPS Office suite for Windows without offering source code or AGPL-compliant notices, despite downloading the software for commercial distribution.
process_and_outcome: A jury awarded Artifex $420,000 in 2020 for breach of contract. The court upheld AGPL enforceability as a contract, not just copyright license.
involved_parties: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California

example_name: Red Hat v. Psychic Software (GPL Enforcement)
example_background: Red Hat enforced the GPL against Psychic Software, a company distributing Red Hat Linux in appliances without providing source code or allowing modifications as required by the license terms for derivative distributions.
process_and_outcome: Settled in 2002 with Psychic agreeing to comply by distributing sources and supporting GPL obligations. No monetary penalties.
